narrative_blocks.header: Her family's name marked a New York hotel
narrative_blocks.body: Amelia Dyckman Van Doren married Edward Hastings Ripley in 1878, and her family owned the Holland House Hotel he built in New York. The couple raised two daughters who married in a celebrated 1909 double wedding.

narrative_blocks.header: Her family's name marked a New York hotel
narrative_blocks.body: Amelia Dyckman Van Doren married Edward Hastings Ripley in 1878, and her family owned the Holland House Hotel he built in New York. The couple raised two daughters who married in a celebrated 1909 double wedding.

notable.notable_blurb: Union general who led troops into Richmond

narrative_blocks.header: He led the first troops into fallen Richmond
narrative_blocks.body: A Vermont colonel who rose to brevet brigadier general, Ripley commanded the brigade ordered to occupy Richmond after its surrender in April 1865. His men fought the fires, stopped the looting, and restored order in the captured Confederate capital.

narrative_blocks.header: He warned Lincoln of a plot in Richmond
narrative_blocks.body: When Lincoln visited Richmond days after its fall, Ripley learned of an assassination plot and urged him to take greater precautions. Lincoln declined, saying it mattered more to be seen among the people as a sign the war was ending.

narrative_blocks.header: He was captured in the Harper's Ferry surrender
narrative_blocks.body: In September 1862 Ripley was among the twelve thousand men surrendered at Harper's Ferry, the largest Union capitulation of the war. Exchanged months later, he returned to lead the 9th Vermont through Virginia and North Carolina.

narrative_blocks.header: He built marble, railroads, and a hotel
narrative_blocks.body: After the war Ripley ran the family's Vermont marble business, built New York's Holland House Hotel, and developed New Jersey's Raritan River Railroad. He also helped found a steamship line to Brazil and led two Rutland banks.

narrative_blocks.header: His war diary became a published book
narrative_blocks.body: Ripley lectured and wrote on his war years, printing his account of the capture of Richmond in 1907. His diary appeared in 1960 as Vermont General, decades after his death.

cross_connections.display_label: shared the Harper's Ferry garrison of September 1862

military_service.unit: 9th Vermont Infantry; 1st Brigade, 3rd Division, XXIV Corps
military_service.notes: Led the Union occupation of Richmond, April 1865.

narrative_blocks.header: A general's granddaughter who married a Pumpelly
narrative_blocks.body: Amelie Sybil Van Doren Ripley was a daughter of the Civil War general Edward Hastings Ripley. She married Raphael Welles Pumpelly in a celebrated 1909 double wedding at New York's Grace Church.

narrative_blocks.header: Her lavish living drained the family fortune
narrative_blocks.body: Amelie's spending, including gifts to the Christian Science church, helped push the Pumpellys toward ruin by 1918. Her 1925 separation from Raphael Welles, on grounds of infidelity, drew newspaper coverage across several states.

narrative_blocks.header: He lost his fortune, then rebuilt it
narrative_blocks.body: After an extravagant life and a bitter 1925 divorce left him penniless, Raphael Welles briefly took his children to live in a cave on Mount Monadnock. Within a year he had rebuilt the family's finances as a New York stockbroker.

narrative_blocks.header: He built 'Samarkand,' a vast Carolina farm
narrative_blocks.body: Naming it for the Asian city his father had visited, Raphael Welles built Samarkand, a two-thousand-acre showplace in the North Carolina sandhills. He set out twenty-four thousand peach trees and bred horses before failed crops forced him to sell.

narrative_blocks.header: A Bahá'í who spoke for the faith
narrative_blocks.body: From 1938 Raphael Welles lectured on the Bahá'í Faith across New York and as far as Idaho, and in 1948 served as a Bahá'í delegate to a United Nations religious conference. His parents had hosted 'Abdu'l-Bahá in 1912.

cross_connections.display_label: was his father-in-law; Union general who led troops into Richmond

narrative_blocks.header: She crossed the color line in 1935
narrative_blocks.body: A New York socialite by birth, Amelie married the African-American boxer and cabinetmaker John Henry Bates in 1935, when such unions were illegal in many states. As Bahá'ís, the couple held to the faith's teaching of the oneness of humankind.

narrative_blocks.header: From socialite to labor-union poster artist
narrative_blocks.body: Born to wealth, Amelie became a painter who made graphic designs and posters for a labor union. She and John moved among the Black artists and writers of the late Harlem Renaissance.
The family kept an ancestral farm at Fayston, Vermont, where their friend Ralph Ellison stayed during the war. Ellison later credited the visits as a direct spark for his novel Invisible Man.

narrative_blocks.header: She died on a Bahá'í mission abroad
narrative_blocks.body: After teaching the Bahá'í Faith in Haiti, Amelie moved with her family toward Mexico City. She died there suddenly of a coronary embolism in 1951, at the age of forty-one.

narrative_blocks.header: He carried the family's Bahá'í faith forward
narrative_blocks.body: Raphael Ripley Pumpelly, also styled Raphael Pumpelly III, was born in Manhattan in 1911 and took up the family's Bahá'í faith as his father had. He married twice and lived to ninety-four, dying in Los Angeles in 2006.

narrative_blocks.header: He lived a year in a mountain cave
narrative_blocks.body: As a boy Raphael III spent about a year with his father and siblings in a cave on Mount Monadnock after the 1925 divorce. He later settled in California, where his second wife went on to marry a film composer.

narrative_blocks.header: He was born at the Samarkand estate
narrative_blocks.body: The youngest Pumpelly son was born in 1913 at his family's Samarkand estate in the North Carolina sandhills. He married Shirley L'Esperance in 1939 and died at Rutland, Vermont, in 1967.
